title: GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) Compliance Tracking
exclusions: Processor relationships where data processing has not yet commenced and SCCs are in legal review; processors relying on alternative Art.46 mechanisms (BCRs, codes of conduct).
evidence: SCC compliance tracker showing all processor relationships requiring SCCs, execution status, module selection rationale, and supplementary measures documentation links.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.46(2)(c) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.46(2)(c) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.37: GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) Compliance Tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.37' running on gdpr_scc_register.csv, gdpr_processor_register.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test processor engagement requiring SCCs where the SCC execution date field is blank and data processing has commenced; verify the compliance search identifies the unsigned SCC and alerts legal.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test processor engagement with fully executed SCCs (correct modules, signed by both parties) before data processing commencement; verify no compliance alert fires.

dataSources: Contract management system exports, SCC execution records and metadata, supplementary technical measures documentation, and vendor onboarding compliance workflow events
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_scc_register.csv
| eval signed_epoch=strptime(signed_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval scc_age_days=round((now()-signed_epoch)/86400,0)
| lookup gdpr_processor_register.csv processor_name OUTPUT sub_processors_last_changed
| eval sub_change_epoch=strptime(sub_processors_last_changed,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval review_needed=if(sub_change_epoch > signed_epoch OR scc_age_days > 1095, 1, 0)
| where review_needed=1 OR scc_version!="2021"
| table processor_name, scc_version, signed_date, scc_age_days, sub_processors_last_changed, review_needed
description: SCCs must be implemented and kept current with module and annex accuracy. This use case monitors SCC version, signing dates, linked processing activities, and triggers review when processor subprocessors or transfer countries change materially.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Record each SCC with version (2021 modules) and signing date; (2) sync sub-processor change dates from vendor notifications; (3) alert legal when sub-processor changes post-date the SCC signature; (4) schedule triennial SCC review tasks; (5) archive signed PDFs externally with URLs in register. Lookups: `gdpr_processor_register`, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`, `gdpr_scc_register`, `gdpr_scc_register.csv`.
visualization: Table (SCCs needing review), Single value (outdated SCC count), Timeline (signature vs sub-processor changes), Bar chart (by module type). Key fields: `processor_name`, `review_needed`, `scc_age_days`, `scc_version`, `signed_date`, `signed_epoch`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **SCC version transition periods** — Organisations transitioning from old SCCs to new EC 2021/914 SCCs within the regulatory deadline may show mixed SCC versions during the migration window.

2. **Multi-module SCC complexity** — Transfers requiring multiple SCC modules (controller-to-processor and processor-to-processor) may show partial completion while all modules are being executed.

3. **SCC supplementary measures** — SCCs requiring supplementary technical or organisational measures per Schrems II may show as incomplete if only the contractual clauses are tracked without the supplementary measures inventory.

4. **Inter-company SCC management** — SCCs between group companies managed centrally may not appear in individual entity compliance tracking systems.

5. **SCC execution timing for new processors** — New processor engagements may have SCCs in legal review during the contracting period with data processing not yet commenced.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_processor_register.csv`, `gdpr_scc_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_processor_register.csv','gdpr_scc_register.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.46(2)(c)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.46(2)(c) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.37: GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) Compliance Tracking." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Record each SCC with version (2021 modules) and signing date. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** sync sub-processor change dates from vendor notifications. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert legal when sub-processor changes post-date the SCC signature. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** schedule triennial SCC review tasks. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** archive signed PDFs externally with URLs in register. Lookups: `gdpr_processor_register`, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`, `gdpr_scc_register`, `gdpr_scc_register.csv`. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) Compliance Tracking," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search begins with `| inputlookup gdpr_scc_register.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `gdpr_scc_register.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup gdpr_scc_register.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true gdpr_scc_register_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.37: GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) Compliance Tracking]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.37,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `processor_name`, `review_needed`, `scc_age_days`, `scc_version`, `signed_date`, `signed_epoch`, `sub_change_epoch`, `sub_processors_last_changed`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table processor_name, review_needed, scc_age_days, scc_version, signed_date, signed_epoch, sub_change_epoch, sub_processors_last_changed
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_processor_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_processor_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `scc_age_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(scc_age_days) as p50 perc90(scc_age_days) as p90 perc99(scc_age_days) as p99 max(scc_age_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `1095` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.37: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`SCCs needing review`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`outdated SCC count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Time trend (`signature vs sub-processor changes`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`by module type`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We sCCs must be implemented and kept current with module and annex accuracy so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
